title: Building a Support System When You Don't Trust People
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Building a support system with C-PTSD feels impossible when you don't trust people. Learn a somatic approach to gently rewire your nervous system for connection.
imageDescription: A delicate ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold in the kintsugi style, sits on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light streams in from a nearby window, illuminating the golden cracks and casting a soft glow on the scene, symbolizing the beauty of healing and repair.
htmlContent:
The most brutal paradox of healing is that the very thing you need most ~ connection ~ is the very thing the trauma taught you to fear. We are told, endlessly, that community heals, that we need a support system, that we must reach out. But what happens when the architecture of your own survival is built on a foundation of deep, cellular mistrust? What happens when the hand reaching for you looks exactly like the one that once did you harm?

For those of us who have navigated the fields of complex trauma, the well-meaning advice to simply “find your people” can feel like being told to breathe in a vacuum. It’s not that we don’t want connection; it’s that the body’s alarm system, honed over years of legitimate threat, screams “danger” long before the conscious mind can even whisper “friend.” This isn’t a failure of character. It is the legacy of a nervous system doing its job with exquisite, heartbreaking precision. ## The Deep Architecture of Distrust To understand why building a support system can feel so impossible, we must first look at the raw mechanics of the nervous system.

When we experience chronic threat, particularly in childhood, the brain and body learn a different set of rules for survival. The world is not mapped as a place of potential joy and connection, but as a minefield of potential dangers. Every interaction is scanned for subtle cues of betrayal, abandonment, or harm.

In his life’s work on somatic experiencing, researcher Peter Levine illuminates how trauma isn’t just an event that happened in the past; it is the frozen, uncompleted energy of that event still living in the body. That energy is what fuels the hypervigilance, the social anxiety, and the striking, bone-deep sense that it is simply not safe to let anyone in. We are not broken; we are brilliantly adapted to a world that, for us, was genuinely unsafe. This rewiring happens at a level far beneath our conscious thoughts, in the primitive parts of the brain responsible for survival.

The amygdala, the brain's smoke detector, becomes chronically overactive, flagging threats where none exist. The prefrontal cortex, responsible for rational thought and impulse control, gets repeatedly overridden. We don't just *think* we're in danger; our entire physiology is screaming it from the rooftops. This adaptation isn’t a cognitive choice.

It’s a physiological reality. The vagus nerve, the superhighway of the nervous system, learns to default to a state of high alert or shutdown. The social engagement system, the very network that is supposed to help us feel safe and connected with others, goes offline in favor of more primitive survival responses. A client once described this to me as feeling like she was a walking, talking exposed nerve ending... the slightest unexpected social friction felt like a physical blow. We might crave intimacy, but the body, in its infinite and sometimes misguided wisdom, vetoes the very possibility of it.

We search for the flawless therapist, the endlessly patient partner, the friendship that requires no explanation. This search, while understandable, is a form of intellectual bypassing. It places the responsibility for our safety outside of ourselves, reinforcing the very pattern of dependency that trauma so often creates. The truth is that no one can give you a felt sense of safety.

That is an inside job. Other people can co-regulate with you, they can offer kindness, they can be trustworthy ~ but the final, non-negotiable experience of safety has to be generated from within your own nervous system. That becomes the hard-won prize of healing: the unshakable knowing that you can handle whatever comes your way, because you have built the internal resources to do so. It turns out to be why so many attempts at connection fail.

We enter into relationships, consciously or not, with a silent demand: “Make me feel safe.” When the other person, inevitably, fails to meet this impossible standard - because they are human, because they have their own triggers, because they cannot live inside our nervous system - the original wound of mistrust is confirmed. See? No one is safe.

Let that land for a moment.

The prophecy fulfills itself. The work is not to find the perfect ally, but to become a better ally to ourselves. It is to learn to stay present with the discomfort of connection, to tolerate the inevitable moments of rupture and repair, and to build, moment by moment, the internal capacity to soothe our own activation.

The concept of titration, borrowed from chemistry, is deeply applicable here. Instead of flooding the system with overwhelming social interaction, we introduce tiny, manageable drops of it. This might look like five minutes of conversation with a barista, a brief, low-stakes text exchange with an acquaintance, or even just making eye contact with a stranger on the street. The goal is not to have a perfect, natural interaction.

What we find here is the work that Peter Levine speaks of when he talks about completing the stress response. We are, in essence, teaching the body, one tiny experience at a time, that it can handle the charge of connection and return to a state of equilibrium. It is a slow, painstaking process, but it is the only one that creates genuine, lasting change. It is not about thinking our way into trust; it is about feeling our way into it.

This is a core tenet of somatic experiencing and a powerful tool for healing. It might involve practices like placing a hand on your heart and feeling the warmth, or noticing the sensation of your feet on the ground. These simple acts of embodiment can be strikingly regulating, sending a powerful signal of safety to the nervous system. ## The Role of Rupture and Repair No relationship, no matter how healthy, is free from moments of misunderstanding, disappointment, or conflict.

Learning to navigate repair is perhaps one of the most advanced skills in healing from complex trauma. It requires us to tolerate the genuine discomfort of being in conflict, to communicate our experience without blame, and to be open to the other person’s perspective. It also requires us to be discerning.

Not all ruptures can or should be repaired. Part of building a healthy support system is learning to distinguish between a healthy relationship with occasional, repairable ruptures and a toxic relationship characterized by a chronic pattern of harm. A helpful question to ask is: “Is this person willing and able to engage in repair with me?” If the answer is no, then that is crucial information. It is a sign that this may not be a safe harbor for your nervous system.

It turns out to be the ultimate act of self-reliance, not as a defense against the world, but as a foundation for engaging with it more fully. You can explore this further in our guide to understanding Internal Family Systems. This is the process from codependence to interdependence, from seeking rescue to seeking partnership.

It is the practice of turning toward, again and again, even when every cell in your body is screaming to turn away. It is the work of a lifetime, and it is not done alone, even when it feels that way. It is done in the quiet moments of self-regulation, in the tentative extension of a hand, in the brave decision to believe in the possibility of repair.

It is the slow, incremental dawn of belonging, not as a place you arrive at, but as a space you learn to cultivate, both within and without. It is a testament to the enduring power of the human spirit to heal and to love, even after the deepest of betrayals. We do not heal from trauma by erasing the past, but by building a present that is strong enough to hold it.

Bear with me on this one.

A present where connection is no longer a threat, but a resource. A present where the body can finally, after so long, come to rest. It is the patient, persistent work of teaching an old system a new truth: that it is possible to be both safe and seen, that vulnerability can be a strength, and that the deepest healing happens not in isolation, but in the gentle, tentative, and ultimately life-changing dance of connection.

What we find here is the tender unfolding of a life that was always waiting for you, just beneath the surface of the fear. It is the homecoming you have been longing for, the one you are creating for yourself, one breath at a time. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I know if I can trust someone? Trust is not a binary switch, but a gradual process of observation and experience. Instead of asking “Can I trust this person?” start by asking “Is this person behaving in a trustworthy way?” Look for consistency over time.

Do their actions align with their words? Are they able to take responsibility and engage in repair when ruptures happen? Trust is built not in grand gestures, but in the accumulation of small, consistent, and reliable actions. It's an assessment your body will make more accurately than your mind.

Pay attention to the subtle cues, the gut feelings, the moments of ease and dis-ease. Your body is a powerful instrument of perception; learn to listen to its wisdom. ### What if I don’t have anyone in my life to practice with? If your current environment lacks safe people to practice with, the work begins with building a support system within yourself and through low-stakes interactions. This can mean developing a strong somatic practice to increase your own capacity for self-regulation, so you feel less dependent on others for a sense of calm. You can also practice ‘titrating trust’ in very small ways, like making brief, friendly eye contact with a cashier or having a short, surface-level conversation with a neighbor.

These micro-interactions can slowly help the nervous system learn that social engagement doesn't always equal threat, creating a foundation for more meaningful connections down the line. The goal is not to force connection, but to gently expand your capacity for it, at a pace that feels sustainable for your system. So what are you going to do about it? For further reading and professional resources, visit the American Psychological Association.
